Records indicate descendants served administrative roles and were acknowledged in later periods. Oda Hihafuri Shingō, noted in the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書}}, and Oda Hihafuri Tokutōji, mentioned in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}, highlight their continued prominence.
Additionally, the Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō appears connected to {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}} as a clan deity, with the Oda Hihafuri family suspected as early ritual practitioners.<ref>Katori Shrine’s origins link to these clans.</ref>
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Raijin Shrine (Asahi City)|ja|雷神社 (旭市)}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/kenkyu/boso/simo.html Ancient Tumuli of Shimotsu Unakami Kuni]

The "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}" also identifies {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天之菩卑能命|1=Ame no Hohi}}’s child, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|建比良鳥命}}, as the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō.<ref>The "Kojiki" notes that Takehiratori no Mikoto was the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō, among others, such as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} and the {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国造}}.</ref>
This region boasted the {{ill|Kuriyama River|ja|栗山川}} and the {{ill|Tsubakimi Lake|ja|椿海}} water systems, yielding 80 dugout canoes—40% of Japan’s total excavated examples—and controlled waterways on the {{ill|Katori Sea|ja|香取海}}, suggesting its significant influence. Between the 4th and 5th centuries, prominent burial mounds such as {{ill|Shakushi Tumulus|ja|しゃくし塚古墳}} and {{ill|Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Tumulus|ja|三ノ分目大塚山古墳}} were constructed.<ref>The use of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} and {{ill|stone coffins|ja|石棺}} in these tumuli indicates high-ranking individuals.</ref><ref>Examples of Nagamochigata stone coffins, considered royal burials, are rare in the Kantō region.</ref> Along with {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko}}, the Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko likely controlled an expansive area described as "Ō Unakami Kuni," stretching across modern Chiba, Ibaraki, Saitama, and Tokyo.
By the 6th century, the {{ill|Shiroyama No. 1 Tumulus (Katori)|ja|城山1号墳 (香取市)}} and {{ill|Omaekizuka Tumulus|ja|御前鬼塚古墳}} were built, while the {{ill|Kinouchi Temple Ruins|ja|木内廃寺跡}} appeared during the transition from the Kokuzō system to the Ritsuryō system in the 7th century.
== Genealogy == The Kokuzō lineage includes the Hisatsuki family ({{ill|Kabane|ja|カバネ}}: {{ill|Atai|ja|直}}) and the Oda Hihafuri family (also titled Atai). Records indicate descendants served administrative roles and were acknowledged in later periods. Oda Hihafuri Shingō, noted in the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書}}, and Oda Hihafuri Tokutōji, mentioned in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}, highlight their continued prominence.
Additionally, the Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō appears connected to {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}} as a clan deity, with the Oda Hihafuri family suspected as early ritual practitioners.<ref>Katori Shrine’s origins link to these clans.</ref>

Between the 4th and 5th centuries, prominent Kofuns such as {{ill|Shakushi Kofun|ja|しゃくし塚古墳}} and {{ill|Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|三ノ分目大塚山古墳}} were constructed.<ref>The use of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} and {{ill|stone coffins|ja|石棺}} in these tumuli indicates high-ranking individuals.</ref><ref>Examples of Nagamochigata stone coffins, considered royal burials, are rare in the Kantō region.</ref> Along with {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko}}, the Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko likely controlled an expansive area described as "Ō Unakami Kuni," stretching across modern Chiba, Ibaraki, Saitama, and Tokyo.
By the 6th century, the {{ill|Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|ja|城山1号墳 (香取市)}} and {{ill|Omaekizuka Kofun|ja|御前鬼塚古墳}} were built, while the {{ill|Kinouchi Temple Ruins|ja|木内廃寺跡}} appeared during the transition from the Kokuzō system to the Ritsuryō system in the 7th century.
== Genealogy == The Kokuzō lineage includes the Hisatsuki family ({{ill|Kabane|ja|カバネ}}: {{ill|Atai|ja|直}}) and the Oda Hihafuri family (also titled Atai). Records indicate descendants served administrative roles and were acknowledged in later periods. Oda Hihafuri Shingō, noted in the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書}}, and Oda Hihafuri Tokutōji, mentioned in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}, highlight their continued prominence.
Additionally, the Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō appears connected to {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}} as a clan deity, with the Oda Hihafuri family suspected as early ritual practitioners.<ref>Katori Shrine’s origins link to these clans.</ref>
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Raijin Shrine (Asahi City)|ja|雷神社 (旭市)}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/kenkyu/boso/simo.html Ancient Tumuli of Shimotsu Unakami Kuni]

revision.text:
'''Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (下海上国造, also known as Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō) governed the eastern portion of the later {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} of {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}}, covering present-day {{ill|Chōshi|ja|銚子市}}, {{ill|Asahi|ja|旭市}}, and {{ill|Katori District|ja|香取郡}} in {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}. The title is also written as 下菟上国造.
== Overview == According to the "{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}" ("Record of Ancient Matters"), the position of Kokuzō was established during the reign of Emperor {{ill|Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} when Hisatsuki no Atae, a descendant of the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko}}, was appointed as Kokuzō. The "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}" also identifies {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天之菩卑能命|1=Ame no Hohi}}’s child, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|建比良鳥命}}, as the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō.<ref>The "Kojiki" notes that Takehiratori no Mikoto was the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō, among others, such as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} and the {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国造}}.</ref>
This region boasted the {{ill|Kuriyama River|ja|栗山川}} and the {{ill|Tsubakimi Lake|ja|椿海}} water systems, yielding 80 dugout canoes—40% of Japan’s total excavated examples—and controlled waterways on the {{ill|Katori Sea|ja|香取海}}, suggesting its significant influence. Between the 4th and 5th centuries, prominent Kofuns such as {{ill|Shakushi Kofun|ja|しゃくし塚古墳}} and {{ill|Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|三ノ分目大塚山古墳}} were constructed.<ref>The use of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} and {{ill|stone coffins|ja|石棺}} in these tumuli indicates high-ranking individuals.</ref><ref>Examples of Nagamochigata stone coffins, considered royal burials, are rare in the Kantō region.</ref> Along with {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko}}, the Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko likely controlled an expansive area described as "Ō Unakami Kuni," stretching across modern Chiba, Ibaraki, Saitama, and Tokyo.
By the 6th century, the {{ill|Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|ja|城山1号墳 (香取市)}} and {{ill|Omaekizuka Kofun|ja|御前鬼塚古墳}} were built, while the {{ill|Kinouchi Temple Ruins|ja|木内廃寺跡}} appeared during the transition from the Kokuzō system to the Ritsuryō system in the 7th century.
== Genealogy == The Kokuzō lineage includes the Hisatsuki family ({{ill|Kabane|ja|カバネ}}: {{ill|Atai|ja|直}}) and the Oda Hihafuri family (also titled Atai). Records indicate descendants served administrative roles and were acknowledged in later periods. Oda Hihafuri Shingō, noted in the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書}}, and Oda Hihafuri Tokutōji, mentioned in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}, highlight their continued prominence.
Additionally, the Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō appears connected to {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}} as a clan deity, with the Oda Hihafuri family suspected as early ritual practitioners.<ref>Katori Shrine’s origins link to these clans.</ref>
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Raijin Shrine (Asahi City)|ja|雷神社 (旭市)}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/kenkyu/boso/simo.html Ancient Tumuli of Shimotsu Unakami Kuni]

Records indicate descendants served administrative roles and were acknowledged in later periods. Oda Hihafuri Shingō, noted in the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書}}, and Oda Hihafuri Tokutōji, mentioned in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}, highlight their continued prominence.

Between the 4th and 5th centuries, prominent Kofuns such as {{ill|Shakushi Kofun|ja|しゃくし塚古墳}} and {{ill|Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|三ノ分目大塚山古墳}} were constructed.<ref>The use of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} and {{ill|stone coffins|ja|石棺}} in these tumuli indicates high-ranking individuals.</ref><ref>Examples of Nagamochigata stone coffins, considered royal burials, are rare in the Kantō region.</ref> Along with {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko}}, the Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko likely controlled an expansive area described as "Ō Unakami Kuni," stretching across modern Chiba, Ibaraki, Saitama, and Tokyo.
By the 6th century, the {{ill|Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|ja|城山1号墳 (香取市)}} and {{ill|Omaekizuka Kofun|ja|御前鬼塚古墳}} were built, while the {{ill|Kinouchi Temple Ruins|ja|木内廃寺跡}} appeared during the transition from the Kokuzō system to the Ritsuryō system in the 7th century.
== Genealogy == The Kokuzō lineage includes the Hisatsuki family ({{ill|Kabane|ja|カバネ}}: {{ill|Atai|ja|直}}) and the Oda Hihafuri family (also titled Atai). Records indicate descendants served administrative roles and were acknowledged in later periods. Oda Hihafuri Shingō, noted in the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書}}, and Oda Hihafuri Tokutōji, mentioned in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}, highlight their continued prominence.
Additionally, the Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō appears connected to {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}} as a clan deity, with the Oda Hihafuri family suspected as early ritual practitioners.<ref>Katori Shrine’s origins link to these clans.</ref>
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Raijin Shrine (Asahi City)|ja|雷神社 (旭市)}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/kenkyu/boso/simo.html Ancient Tumuli of Shimotsu Unakami Kuni]

'''Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (下海上国造, also known as Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō) governed the eastern portion of the later {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} of {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}}, covering present-day {{ill|Chōshi|ja|銚子市}}, {{ill|Asahi|ja|旭市}}, and {{ill|Katori District|ja|香取郡}} in {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}. The title is also written as 下菟上国造.
== Overview == According to the "{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}" ("Record of Ancient Matters"), the position of Kokuzō was established during the reign of Emperor {{ill|Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}} when Hisatsuki no Atae, a descendant of the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko}}, was appointed as Kokuzō. The "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}" also identifies {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天之菩卑能命|1=Ame no Hohi}}’s child, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|建比良鳥命}}, as the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō.<ref>The "Kojiki" notes that Takehiratori no Mikoto was the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō, among others, such as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造}} and the {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国造}}.</ref>
This region boasted the {{ill|Kuriyama River|ja|栗山川}} and the {{ill|Tsubakimi Lake|ja|椿海}} water systems, yielding 80 dugout canoes—40% of Japan’s total excavated examples—and controlled waterways on the {{ill|Katori Sea|ja|香取海}}, suggesting its significant influence. Between the 4th and 5th centuries, prominent Kofuns such as {{ill|Shakushi Kofun|ja|しゃくし塚古墳}} and {{ill|Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|三ノ分目大塚山古墳}} were constructed.<ref>The use of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} and {{ill|stone coffins|ja|石棺}} in these tumuli indicates high-ranking individuals.</ref><ref>Examples of Nagamochigata stone coffins, considered royal burials, are rare in the Kantō region.</ref> Along with {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko}}, the Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko likely controlled an expansive area described as "Ō Unakami Kuni," stretching across modern Chiba, Ibaraki, Saitama, and Tokyo.
By the 6th century, the {{ill|Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|ja|城山1号墳 (香取市)}} and {{ill|Omaekizuka Kofun|ja|御前鬼塚古墳}} were built, while the {{ill|Kinouchi Temple Ruins|ja|木内廃寺跡}} appeared during the transition from the Kokuzō system to the Ritsuryō system in the 7th century.
== Genealogy == The Kokuzō lineage includes the Hisatsuki family ({{ill|Kabane|ja|カバネ}}: {{ill|Atai|ja|直}}) and the Oda Hihafuri family (also titled Atai). Records indicate descendants served administrative roles and were acknowledged in later periods. Oda Hihafuri Shingō, noted in the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書}}, and Oda Hihafuri Tokutōji, mentioned in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}, highlight their continued prominence.
Additionally, the Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō appears connected to {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}} as a clan deity, with the Oda Hihafuri family suspected as early ritual practitioners.<ref>Katori Shrine’s origins link to these clans.</ref>
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Raijin Shrine (Asahi City)|ja|雷神社 (旭市)}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/kenkyu/boso/simo.html Ancient Tumuli of Shimotsu Unakami Kuni]

The title is also written as 下菟上国造.

The "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}" also identifies {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天之菩卑能命|1=Ame no Hohi}}’s child, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|zh|建比良鳥命|fr}}, as the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō.<ref>The "Kojiki" notes that Takehiratori no Mikoto was the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō, among others, such as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造}} and the {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国造}}.</ref>
This region boasted the {{ill|Kuriyama River|ja|栗山川}} and the {{ill|Tsubakimi Lake|ja|椿海}} water systems, yielding 80 dugout canoes—40% of Japan’s total excavated examples—and controlled waterways on the {{ill|Katori Sea|ja|香取海}}, suggesting its significant influence. Between the 4th and 5th centuries, prominent Kofuns such as {{ill|Shakushi Kofun|ja|しゃくし塚古墳}} and {{ill|Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|三ノ分目大塚山古墳}} were constructed.<ref>The use of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} and {{ill|stone coffins|ja|石棺}} in these tumuli indicates high-ranking individuals.</ref><ref>Examples of Nagamochigata stone coffins, considered royal burials, are rare in the Kantō region.</ref> Along with {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko}}, the Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko likely controlled an expansive area described as "Ō Unakami Kuni," stretching across modern Chiba, Ibaraki, Saitama, and Tokyo.
By the 6th century, the {{ill|Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|ja|城山1号墳 (香取市)}} and {{ill|Omaekizuka Kofun|ja|御前鬼塚古墳}} were built, while the {{ill|Kinouchi Temple Ruins|ja|木内廃寺跡}} appeared during the transition from the Kokuzō system to the Ritsuryō system in the 7th century.
== Genealogy == The Kokuzō lineage includes the Hisatsuki family ({{ill|Kabane|ja|カバネ}}: {{ill|Atai|ja|直}}) and the Oda Hihafuri family (also titled Atai). Records indicate descendants served administrative roles and were acknowledged in later periods. Oda Hihafuri Shingō, noted in the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書}}, and Oda Hihafuri Tokutōji, mentioned in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}, highlight their continued prominence.
Additionally, the Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō appears connected to {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}} as a clan deity, with the Oda Hihafuri family suspected as early ritual practitioners.<ref>Katori Shrine’s origins link to these clans.</ref>
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Raijin Shrine (Asahi City)|ja|雷神社 (旭市)}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/kenkyu/boso/simo.html Ancient Tumuli of Shimotsu Unakami Kuni]

By the 6th century, the {{ill|Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|ja|城山1号墳 (香取市)|12=draft}} and {{ill|Omaekizuka Kofun|ja|御前鬼塚古墳|12=draft}} were built, while the {{ill|Kinouchi Temple Ruins|ja|木内廃寺跡|12=draft}} appeared during the transition from the Kokuzō system to the Ritsuryō system in the 7th century.

Records indicate descendants served administrative roles and were acknowledged in later periods. Oda Hihafuri Shingō, noted in the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=draft}}, and Oda Hihafuri Tokutōji, mentioned in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集|12=draft}}, highlight their continued prominence.

revision.text:
'''下海上国造'''（しもつうなかみのくにみやつこ・しもつうなかみこくぞう）は、後の[[令制国]]の[[下総国]]東部、現在の[[千葉県]][[銚子市]]、[[旭市]]及び[[香取郡]]一帯を支配した[[国造]]。下菟上国造とも。
== 解説 == 『[[先代旧事本紀]]』「国造本紀」によれば、[[応神天皇]]の御世に[[上海上国造]]祖の孫久都伎直を国造に定められたとされる。また『古事記』では、[[アメノホヒ|天之菩卑能命]]の子[[建比良鳥命]]を下菟上国造の祖とする<ref>『古事記』には、建比良鳥命、此は[[出雲国造]]、[[武蔵国造|无邪志国造]]、[[上海上国造|上菟上国造]]、下菟上国造、[[伊甚国造|伊自牟国造]]、[[津島県直]]、[[遠江国造]]等の祖なり、とある。</ref>。
領域には日本全体の40パーセントに相当する80例もの[[丸木舟]]を出土した[[栗山川]]／[[椿海]]水系があり、[[香取海]]の[[水上交通]]を制した有力勢力だったと考えられている。[[4世紀]]から[[5世紀]]には[[しゃくし塚古墳]]や[[三ノ分目大塚山古墳]]が造営され<ref>特に三ノ分目大塚山古墳の[[石棺]]は、大王の[[棺]]とされる[[長持形石棺]]であり、被葬者の地位の高さを示している。</ref><ref>関東地方での長持形石棺の例は、三ノ分目大塚山古墳の他、千葉県[[木更津市]]の[[高柳銚子塚古墳]]、[[群馬県]][[伊勢崎市]]の[[お富士山古墳]]、同県[[太田市]]の[[太田天神山古墳]]の3例のみである。</ref>、[[上海上国造]]と合わせ[[千葉県]]中部から[[茨城県]]、[[埼玉県]]、[[東京都]]にかけての一帯を支配した「大海上国」ともいうべき勢力圏があったとする説もある。その後[[6世紀]]には[[城山1号墳 (香取市)|城山1号墳]]や[[御前鬼塚古墳]]が、[[7世紀]]の国造制から[[律令制]]への移行期には初期寺院[[木内廃寺跡|木内廃寺]]が建立されている。
なお、当国造の領域と上海上国造の領域の間に[[武社国造]]の領域がはいっていることから、房総の国造制の展開には少なくとも2段階があったとされ、6世紀に中央から進出した勢力の建てた武社国によって「大海上国」は上・下に分割され、香取海上流に[[印波国造]]が進出、さらに[[千葉国造]]に続き[[匝瑳郡]]が建てられ領域を割かれたとされている<ref>「国造本紀」には無いが、『日本後紀』に「千葉国造大私部直善人」の名がみえ、『続日本後紀』に匝瑳郡の建郡についての記事がある。</ref>。
国造氏族は、「国造本紀」にある久都伎氏（[[カバネ]]は[[直 (姓)|直]]）の他、他田日奉氏（同じくカバネは直）が知られる。下海上国造の後裔を称する[[他田日奉神護]]は、[[正倉院文書]]に「他田日奉部直神護解」を遺し、他田日奉得大理は、[[万葉集]]、巻二十 防人歌に、「暁の かはたれ時に 島蔭を 漕ぎ去し船の たづき知らずも」と詠んでいる。また、『[[続日本紀]]』[[延暦]]4年（785年）正月27日条では、他田日奉徳刀自に[[外位|外]][[従五位下]]の位階が授けられ、『[[日本三代実録]]』[[仁和]]元年（[[885年]]）閏3月19日の条によれば、海上郡大領の他田日奉春岳が、百姓の調庸を代納した功績により外従五位下を授けられている。その他、[[香取神宮]]は下海上国造の[[氏神]]であり、国造を担った他田日奉氏を香取神宮の原始祭祀氏族と推測し、香取氏はその支配下にあったと見る説もある。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 加藤謙吉 他『日本古代史地名事典』 雄山閣、2007年、ISBN 978-4-639-01995-4、238頁 * 石井進 他『千葉県の歴史』 山川出版社 2000年。ISBN 4-634-32120-3、30頁
== 関連項目 == * [[雷神社 (旭市)]]
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/kenkyu/boso/simo.html 房総の古墳を歩く 下海上国の古墳]
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しもつうなかみのくにのみやつこ}} [[Category:下総国]] [[Category:国造]] [[Category:千葉県の歴史]]

なお、当国造の領域と上海上国造の領域の間に[[武社国造]]の領域がはいっていることから、房総の国造制の展開には少なくとも2段階があったとされ、6世紀に中央から進出した勢力の建てた武社国によって「大海上国」は上・下に分割され、香取海上流に[[印波国造]]が進出、さらに[[千葉国造]]に続き[[匝瑳郡]]が建てられ領域を割かれたとされている<ref>「国造本紀」には無いが、『日本後紀』に「千葉国造大私部直善人」の名がみえ、『続日本後紀』に匝瑳郡の建郡についての記事がある。</ref>。

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:下海上国造]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11361123]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (下海上国造, also known as Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō) governed the eastern portion of the later [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] of [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]], covering present-day [[:en:Chōshi|Chōshi]], [[:en:Asahi, Chiba|Asahi]], and [[:en:Katori District|Katori District]] in [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]]. The title is also written as 下菟上国造.
== Overview == According to the "[[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]]" ("Record of Ancient Matters"), the position of Kokuzō was established during the reign of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Ōjin]] when Hisatsuki no Atae, a descendant of the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}}, was appointed as Kokuzō. The "[[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]" also identifies {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天之菩卑能命|1=Ame no Hohi|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hohi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノホヒ}}’s child, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|1=Takehi-Nateru|zh|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|fr|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehiratori no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|qq=}}, as the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō.<ref>The "Kojiki" notes that Takehiratori no Mikoto was the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō, among others, such as the {{ill|Izumo clan|lt=Izumo clan|lt=Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]].</ref>
This region boasted the [[:en:Kuriyama River|Kuriyama River]] and the {{ill|Tsubakimi Lake|ja|椿海|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsubakimi Lake|qq=}} water systems, yielding 80 dugout canoes—40% of Japan’s total excavated examples—and controlled waterways on the [[:en:Katori Sea|Katori Sea]], suggesting its significant influence. Between the 4th and 5th centuries, prominent Kofuns such as {{ill|Shakushi Kofun|ja|しゃくし塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakushi Kofun|qq=|1=Shakushi Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11269068}} and {{ill|Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|三ノ分目大塚山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|qq=}} were constructed.<ref>The use of [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] and {{ill|Sarcophaguss|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarcophaguss|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}} in these tumuli indicates high-Ranking individuals.</ref><ref>Examples of Nagamochigata Sarcophaguss, considered royal burials, are rare in the Kantō region.</ref> Along with {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}}, the Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko likely controlled an expansive area described as "Ō Unakami Kuni," stretching across modern Chiba, Ibaraki, Saitama, and Tokyo.
By the 6th century, the {{ill|Joyama Tomb No.1 (Katori)|lt=Joyama Tomb No.1 (Katori)|lt=Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|ja|城山1号墳 (香取市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|qq=}} and {{ill|Omaekizuka Kofun|ja|御前鬼塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omaekizuka Kofun|qq=|1=Omaekizuka Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488824}} were built, while the {{ill|Kinouchi Temple Ruins|ja|木内廃寺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinouchi Temple Ruins|qq=}} appeared during the transition from the Kokuzō system to the Ritsuryō system in the 7th century.
== Genealogy == The Kokuzō lineage includes the Hisatsuki family ([[:en:Kabane|Kabane]]: {{ill|Atai|ja|直|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atai|qq=|1=Atai (kabane)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581705}}) and the Oda Hihafuri family (also titled Atai). Records indicate descendants served administrative roles and were acknowledged in later periods. Oda Hihafuri Shingō, noted in the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsōin documents|qq=|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, and Oda Hihafuri Tokutōji, mentioned in the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]], highlight their continued prominence.
Additionally, the Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō appears connected to [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]] as a clan deity, with the Oda Hihafuri family suspected as early ritual practitioners.<ref>Katori Shrine’s origins link to these clans.</ref>
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Raijin Shrine (Asahi City)|ja|雷神社 (旭市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raijin Shrine (Asahi City)|qq=}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/kenkyu/boso/simo.html Ancient Tumuli of Shimotsu Unakami Kuni]

The title is also written as 下菟上国造.
== Overview == According to the "[[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]]" ("Record of Ancient Matters"), the position of Kokuzō was established during the reign of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Ōjin]] when Hisatsuki no Atae, a descendant of the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}}, was appointed as Kokuzō. The "[[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]" also identifies {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天之菩卑能命|1=Ame no Hohi|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hohi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノホヒ}}’s child, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|1=Takehi-Nateru|zh|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|fr|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehiratori no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|qq=}}, as the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō.<ref>The "Kojiki" notes that Takehiratori no Mikoto was the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō, among others, such as the {{ill|Izumo clan|lt=Izumo clan|lt=Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]].</ref>
This region boasted the [[:en:Kuriyama River|Kuriyama River]] and the {{ill|Tsubakimi Lake|ja|椿海|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsubakimi Lake|qq=}} water systems, yielding 80 dugout canoes—40% of Japan’s total excavated examples—and controlled waterways on the [[:en:Katori Sea|Katori Sea]], suggesting its significant influence. Between the 4th and 5th centuries, prominent Kofuns such as {{ill|Shakushi Kofun|ja|しゃくし塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakushi Kofun|1=Shakushi Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11269068}} and {{ill|Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|三ノ分目大塚山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|qq=}} were constructed.<ref>The use of [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] and {{ill|Sarcophaguss|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarcophaguss|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}} in these tumuli indicates high-Ranking individuals.</ref><ref>Examples of Nagamochigata Sarcophaguss, considered royal burials, are rare in the Kantō region.</ref> Along with {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}}, the Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko likely controlled an expansive area described as "Ō Unakami Kuni," stretching across modern Chiba, Ibaraki, Saitama, and Tokyo.
By the 6th century, the {{ill|Joyama Tomb No.1 (Katori)|lt=Joyama Tomb No.1 (Katori)|lt=Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|ja|城山1号墳 (香取市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|qq=}} and {{ill|Omaekizuka Kofun|ja|御前鬼塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omaekizuka Kofun|1=Omaekizuka Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488824}} were built, while the {{ill|Kinouchi Temple Ruins|ja|木内廃寺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinouchi Temple Ruins|qq=}} appeared during the transition from the Kokuzō system to the Ritsuryō system in the 7th century.
== Genealogy == The Kokuzō lineage includes the Hisatsuki family ([[:en:Kabane|Kabane]]: {{ill|Atai|ja|直|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atai|1=Atai (kabane)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581705}}) and the Oda Hihafuri family (also titled Atai). Records indicate descendants served administrative roles and were acknowledged in later periods. Oda Hihafuri Shingō, noted in the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsōin documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, and Oda Hihafuri Tokutōji, mentioned in the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]], highlight their continued prominence.
Additionally, the Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō appears connected to [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]] as a clan deity, with the Oda Hihafuri family suspected as early ritual practitioners.<ref>Katori Shrine’s origins link to these clans.</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:下海上国造]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11361123]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (下海上国造, also known as Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō) governed the eastern portion of the later [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] of [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]], covering present-day [[:en:Chōshi|Chōshi]], [[:en:Asahi, Chiba|Asahi]], and [[:en:Katori District|Katori District]] in [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]]. The title is also written as 下菟上国造.
== Overview == According to the "[[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]]" ("Record of Ancient Matters"), the position of Kokuzō was established during the reign of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Ōjin]] when Hisatsuki no Atae, a descendant of the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}}, was appointed as Kokuzō. The "[[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]" also identifies {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天之菩卑能命|1=Ame no Hohi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hohi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノホヒ}}’s child, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|1=Takehi-Nateru|zh|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|fr|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehiratori no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|qq=}}, as the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō.<ref>The "Kojiki" notes that Takehiratori no Mikoto was the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō, among others, such as the {{ill|Izumo clan|lt=Izumo clan|lt=Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]].</ref>
This region boasted the [[:en:Kuriyama River|Kuriyama River]] and the {{ill|Tsubakimi Lake|ja|椿海|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsubakimi Lake|qq=}} water systems, yielding 80 dugout canoes—40% of Japan’s total excavated examples—and controlled waterways on the [[:en:Katori Sea|Katori Sea]], suggesting its significant influence. Between the 4th and 5th centuries, prominent Kofuns such as {{ill|Shakushi Kofun|ja|しゃくし塚古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakushi Kofun|1=Shakushi Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11269068}} and {{ill|Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|三ノ分目大塚山古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|qq=}} were constructed.<ref>The use of [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] and {{ill|Sarcophaguss|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarcophaguss|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}} in these tumuli indicates high-Ranking individuals.</ref><ref>Examples of Nagamochigata Sarcophaguss, considered royal burials, are rare in the Kantō region.</ref> Along with {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}}, the Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko likely controlled an expansive area described as "Ō Unakami Kuni," stretching across modern Chiba, Ibaraki, Saitama, and Tokyo.
By the 6th century, the {{ill|Joyama Tomb No.1 (Katori)|lt=Joyama Tomb No.1 (Katori)|lt=Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|ja|城山1号墳 (香取市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|qq=}} and {{ill|Omaekizuka Kofun|ja|御前鬼塚古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omaekizuka Kofun|1=Omaekizuka Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488824}} were built, while the {{ill|Kinouchi Temple Ruins|ja|木内廃寺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinouchi Temple Ruins|qq=}} appeared during the transition from the Kokuzō system to the Ritsuryō system in the 7th century.
== Genealogy == The Kokuzō lineage includes the Hisatsuki family ([[:en:Kabane|Kabane]]: {{ill|Atai|ja|直|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atai|1=Atai (kabane)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581705}}) and the Oda Hihafuri family (also titled Atai). Records indicate descendants served administrative roles and were acknowledged in later periods. Oda Hihafuri Shingō, noted in the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsōin documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, and Oda Hihafuri Tokutōji, mentioned in the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]], highlight their continued prominence.
Additionally, the Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō appears connected to [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]] as a clan deity, with the Oda Hihafuri family suspected as early ritual practitioners.<ref>Katori Shrine’s origins link to these clans.</ref>
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Raijin Shrine (Asahi City)|ja|雷神社 (旭市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raijin Shrine (Asahi City)|qq=}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:下海上国造]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11361123]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (下海上国造, also known as Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō) governed the eastern portion of the later [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] of [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]], covering present-day [[:en:Chōshi|Chōshi]], [[:en:Asahi, Chiba|Asahi]], and [[:en:Katori District|Katori District]] in [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]]. The title is also written as 下菟上国造.
== Overview == According to the "[[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]]" ("Record of Ancient Matters"), the position of Kokuzō was established during the reign of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Ōjin]] when Hisatsuki no Atae, a descendant of the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}}, was appointed as Kokuzō. The "[[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]" also identifies {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天之菩卑能命|1=Ame no Hohi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hohi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノホヒ}}’s child, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|1=Takehi-Nateru|zh|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehiratori no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|qq=}}, as the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō.<ref>The "Kojiki" notes that Takehiratori no Mikoto was the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō, among others, such as the {{ill|Izumo clan|lt=Izumo clan|lt=Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]].</ref>
This region boasted the [[:en:Kuriyama River|Kuriyama River]] and the {{ill|Tsubakimi Lake|ja|椿海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsubakimi Lake|qq=}} water systems, yielding 80 dugout canoes—40% of Japan’s total excavated examples—and controlled waterways on the [[:en:Katori Sea|Katori Sea]], suggesting its significant influence. Between the 4th and 5th centuries, prominent Kofuns such as {{ill|Shakushi Kofun|ja|しゃくし塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakushi Kofun|1=Shakushi Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11269068}} and {{ill|Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|三ノ分目大塚山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|qq=}} were constructed.<ref>The use of [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] and {{ill|Sarcophaguss|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarcophaguss|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}} in these tumuli indicates high-Ranking individuals.</ref><ref>Examples of Nagamochigata Sarcophaguss, considered royal burials, are rare in the Kantō region.</ref> Along with {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}}, the Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko likely controlled an expansive area described as "Ō Unakami Kuni," stretching across modern Chiba, Ibaraki, Saitama, and Tokyo.
By the 6th century, the {{ill|Joyama Tomb No.1 (Katori)|lt=Joyama Tomb No.1 (Katori)|lt=Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|ja|城山1号墳 (香取市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|qq=}} and {{ill|Omaekizuka Kofun|ja|御前鬼塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omaekizuka Kofun|1=Omaekizuka Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488824}} were built, while the {{ill|Kinouchi Temple Ruins|ja|木内廃寺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinouchi Temple Ruins|qq=}} appeared during the transition from the Kokuzō system to the Ritsuryō system in the 7th century.
== Genealogy == The Kokuzō lineage includes the Hisatsuki family ([[:en:Kabane|Kabane]]: {{ill|Atai|ja|直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atai|1=Atai (kabane)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581705}}) and the Oda Hihafuri family (also titled Atai). Records indicate descendants served administrative roles and were acknowledged in later periods. Oda Hihafuri Shingō, noted in the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsōin documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, and Oda Hihafuri Tokutōji, mentioned in the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]], highlight their continued prominence.
Additionally, the Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō appears connected to [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]] as a clan deity, with the Oda Hihafuri family suspected as early ritual practitioners.<ref>Katori Shrine’s origins link to these clans.</ref>
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Raijin Shrine (Asahi City)|ja|雷神社 (旭市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raijin Shrine (Asahi City)|qq=}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/kenkyu/boso/simo.html Ancient Tumuli of Shimotsu Unakami Kuni]

Oda Hihafuri Shingō, noted in the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsōin documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, and Oda Hihafuri Tokutōji, mentioned in the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]], highlight their continued prominence.
Additionally, the Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō appears connected to [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]] as a clan deity, with the Oda Hihafuri family suspected as early ritual practitioners.<ref>Katori Shrine’s origins link to these clans.</ref>
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Raijin Shrine (Asahi City)|ja|雷神社 (旭市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raijin Shrine (Asahi City)|qq=}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/kenkyu/boso/simo.html Ancient Tumuli of Shimotsu Unakami Kuni]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:下海上国造]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11361123]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (下海上国造, also known as Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō) governed the eastern portion of the later [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] of [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]], covering present-day [[:en:Chōshi|Chōshi]], [[:en:Asahi, Chiba|Asahi]], and [[:en:Katori District|Katori District]] in [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]]. The title is also written as 下菟上国造.
== Overview == According to the "[[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]]" ("Record of Ancient Matters"), the position of Kokuzō was established during the reign of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Ōjin]] when Hisatsuki no Atae, a descendant of the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}}, was appointed as Kokuzō. The "[[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]" also identifies {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天之菩卑能命|1=Ame no Hohi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hohi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノホヒ}}’s child, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|1=Takehi-Nateru|zh|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehiratori no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|qq=}}, as the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō.<ref>The "Kojiki" notes that Takehiratori no Mikoto was the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō, among others, such as the {{ill|Izumo clan|lt=Izumo clan|lt=Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]].</ref>
This region boasted the [[:en:Kuriyama River|Kuriyama River]] and the {{ill|Tsubakimi Lake|ja|椿海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsubakimi Lake|qq=}} water systems, yielding 80 dugout canoes—40% of Japan’s total excavated examples—and controlled waterways on the [[:en:Katori Sea|Katori Sea]], suggesting its significant influence. Between the 4th and 5th centuries, prominent Kofuns such as {{ill|Shakushi Kofun|ja|しゃくし塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakushi Kofun|1=Shakushi Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11269068}} and {{ill|Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|三ノ分目大塚山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|qq=}} were constructed.<ref>The use of [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] and {{ill|Sarcophaguss|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarcophaguss|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}} in these tumuli indicates high-Ranking individuals.</ref><ref>Examples of Nagamochigata Sarcophaguss, considered royal burials, are rare in the Kantō region.</ref> Along with {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}}, the Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko likely controlled an expansive area described as "Ō Unakami Kuni," stretching across modern Chiba, Ibaraki, Saitama, and Tokyo.
By the 6th century, the {{ill|Joyama Tomb No.1 (Katori)|lt=Joyama Tomb No.1 (Katori)|lt=Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|ja|城山1号墳 (香取市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|qq=}} and {{ill|Omaekizuka Kofun|ja|御前鬼塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omaekizuka Kofun|1=Omaekizuka Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488824}} were built, while the {{ill|Kinouchi Temple Ruins|ja|木内廃寺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinouchi Temple Ruins|qq=}} appeared during the transition from the Kokuzō system to the Ritsuryō system in the 7th century.
== Genealogy == The Kokuzō lineage includes the Hisatsuki family ([[:en:Kabane|Kabane]]: {{ill|Atai|ja|直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atai|1=Atai (kabane)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581705}}) and the Oda Hihafuri family (also titled Atai). Records indicate descendants served administrative roles and were acknowledged in later periods. Oda Hihafuri Shingō, noted in the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsōin documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, and Oda Hihafuri Tokutōji, mentioned in the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]], highlight their continued prominence.
Additionally, the Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō appears connected to [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]] as a clan deity, with the Oda Hihafuri family suspected as early ritual practitioners.<ref>Katori Shrine’s origins link to these clans.</ref>
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Raijin Shrine (Asahi City)|ja|雷神社 (旭市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raijin Shrine (Asahi City)|qq=}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/kenkyu/boso/simo.html Ancient Tumuli of Shimotsu Unakami Kuni]

The title is also written as 下菟上国造.
== Overview == According to the "[[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]]" ("Record of Ancient Matters"), the position of Kokuzō was established during the reign of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Ōjin]] when Hisatsuki no Atae, a descendant of the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}}, was appointed as Kokuzō. The "[[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]" also identifies {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天之菩卑能命|1=Ame no Hohi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hohi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノホヒ}}’s child, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|1=Takehi-Nateru|zh|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehiratori no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|qq=}}, as the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō.<ref>The "Kojiki" notes that Takehiratori no Mikoto was the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō, among others, such as the {{ill|Izumo clan|lt=Izumo clan|lt=Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]].</ref>
This region boasted the [[:en:Kuriyama River|Kuriyama River]] and the {{ill|Tsubakimi Lake|ja|椿海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsubakimi Lake|qq=}} water systems, yielding 80 dugout canoes—40% of Japan’s total excavated examples—and controlled waterways on the [[:en:Katori Sea|Katori Sea]], suggesting its significant influence. Between the 4th and 5th centuries, prominent Kofuns such as {{ill|Shakushi Kofun|ja|しゃくし塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakushi Kofun|1=Shakushi Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11269068}} and {{ill|Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|三ノ分目大塚山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|qq=}} were constructed.<ref>The use of [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] and {{ill|Sarcophaguss|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarcophaguss|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}} in these tumuli indicates high-Ranking individuals.</ref><ref>Examples of Nagamochigata Sarcophaguss, considered royal burials, are rare in the Kantō region.</ref> Along with {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}}, the Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko likely controlled an expansive area described as "Ō Unakami Kuni," stretching across modern Chiba, Ibaraki, Saitama, and Tokyo.
By the 6th century, the {{ill|Joyama Tomb No.1 (Katori)|lt=Joyama Tomb No.1 (Katori)|lt=Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|ja|城山1号墳 (香取市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|qq=}} and {{ill|Omaekizuka Kofun|ja|御前鬼塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omaekizuka Kofun|1=Omaekizuka Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488824}} were built, while the {{ill|Kinouchi Temple Ruins|ja|木内廃寺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinouchi Temple Ruins|qq=}} appeared during the transition from the Kokuzō system to the Ritsuryō system in the 7th century.
== Genealogy == The Kokuzō lineage includes the Hisatsuki family ([[:en:Kabane|Kabane]]: {{ill|Atai|ja|直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atai|1=Atai (kabane)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581705}}) and the Oda Hihafuri family (also titled Atai). Records indicate descendants served administrative roles and were acknowledged in later periods. Oda Hihafuri Shingō, noted in the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsōin documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, and Oda Hihafuri Tokutōji, mentioned in the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]], highlight their continued prominence.
Additionally, the Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō appears connected to [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]] as a clan deity, with the Oda Hihafuri family suspected as early ritual practitioners.<ref>Katori Shrine’s origins link to these clans.</ref>
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Raijin Shrine (Asahi City)|ja|雷神社 (旭市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raijin Shrine (Asahi City)|qq=}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/kenkyu/boso/simo.html Ancient Tumuli of Shimotsu Unakami Kuni]

== Genealogy == The Kokuzō lineage includes the Hisatsuki family ([[:en:Kabane|Kabane]]: {{ill|Atai|ja|直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atai|1=Atai (kabane)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581705}}) and the Oda Hihafuri family (also titled Atai). Records indicate descendants served administrative roles and were acknowledged in later periods. Oda Hihafuri Shingō, noted in the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsōin documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, and Oda Hihafuri Tokutōji, mentioned in the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]], highlight their continued prominence.
Additionally, the Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō appears connected to [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]] as a clan deity, with the Oda Hihafuri family suspected as early ritual practitioners.<ref>Katori Shrine’s origins link to these clans.</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:下海上国造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:下海上国造]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11361123]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (下海上国造, also known as Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō) governed the eastern portion of the later [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] of [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]], covering present-day [[:en:Chōshi|Chōshi]], [[:en:Asahi, Chiba|Asahi]], and [[:en:Katori District|Katori District]] in [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]]. The title is also written as 下菟上国造.
== Overview == According to the "[[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]]" ("Record of Ancient Matters"), the position of Kokuzō was established during the reign of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Ōjin]] when Hisatsuki no Atae, a descendant of the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}}, was appointed as Kokuzō. The "[[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]" also identifies {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天之菩卑能命|1=Ame no Hohi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hohi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノホヒ}}’s child, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|1=Takehi-Nateru|zh|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehiratori no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|qq=}}, as the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō.<ref>The "Kojiki" notes that Takehiratori no Mikoto was the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō, among others, such as the {{ill|Izumo clan|lt=Izumo clan|lt=Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]].</ref>
This region boasted the [[:en:Kuriyama River|Kuriyama River]] and the {{ill|Tsubakimi Lake|ja|椿海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsubakimi Lake|qq=}} water systems, yielding 80 dugout canoes—40% of Japan’s total excavated examples—and controlled waterways on the [[:en:Katori Sea|Katori Sea]], suggesting its significant influence. Between the 4th and 5th centuries, prominent Kofuns such as {{ill|Shakushi Kofun|ja|しゃくし塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakushi Kofun|1=Shakushi Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11269068}} and {{ill|Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|三ノ分目大塚山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|qq=}} were constructed.<ref>The use of [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] and {{ill|Sarcophaguss|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarcophaguss|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}} in these tumuli indicates high-Ranking individuals.</ref><ref>Examples of Nagamochigata Sarcophaguss, considered royal burials, are rare in the Kantō region.</ref> Along with {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}}, the Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko likely controlled an expansive area described as "Ō Unakami Kuni," stretching across modern Chiba, Ibaraki, Saitama, and Tokyo.
By the 6th century, the {{ill|Joyama Tomb No.1 (Katori)|lt=Joyama Tomb No.1 (Katori)|lt=Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|ja|城山1号墳 (香取市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|qq=}} and {{ill|Omaekizuka Kofun|ja|御前鬼塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omaekizuka Kofun|1=Omaekizuka Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488824}} were built, while the {{ill|Kinouchi Temple Ruins|ja|木内廃寺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinouchi Temple Ruins|qq=}} appeared during the transition from the Kokuzō system to the Ritsuryō system in the 7th century.
== Genealogy == The Kokuzō lineage includes the Hisatsuki family ([[:en:Kabane|Kabane]]: {{ill|Atai|ja|直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atai|1=Atai (kabane)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581705}}) and the Oda Hihafuri family (also titled Atai). Records indicate descendants served administrative roles and were acknowledged in later periods. Oda Hihafuri Shingō, noted in the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsōin documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, and Oda Hihafuri Tokutōji, mentioned in the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]], highlight their continued prominence.
Additionally, the Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō appears connected to [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]] as a clan deity, with the Oda Hihafuri family suspected as early ritual practitioners.<ref>Katori Shrine’s origins link to these clans.</ref>
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Raijin Shrine (Asahi City)|ja|雷神社 (旭市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raijin Shrine (Asahi City)|qq=}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:下海上国造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:下海上国造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:下海上国造]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11361123]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (下海上国造, also known as Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō) governed the eastern portion of the later [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] of [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]], covering present-day [[:en:Chōshi|Chōshi]], [[:en:Asahi, Chiba|Asahi]], and [[:en:Katori District|Katori District]] in [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]]. The title is also written as 下菟上国造.
== Overview == According to the "[[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]]" ("Record of Ancient Matters"), the position of Kokuzō was established during the reign of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Ōjin]] when Hisatsuki no Atae, a descendant of the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}}, was appointed as Kokuzō. The "[[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]" also identifies {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天之菩卑能命|1=Ame no Hohi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hohi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノホヒ}}’s child, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|1=Takehi-Nateru|zh|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehiratori no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|qq=}}, as the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō.<ref>The "Kojiki" notes that Takehiratori no Mikoto was the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō, among others, such as the {{ill|Izumo clan|lt=Izumo clan|lt=Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]].</ref>
This region boasted the [[:en:Kuriyama River|Kuriyama River]] and the {{ill|Tsubakimi Lake|ja|椿海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsubakimi Lake|qq=}} water systems, yielding 80 dugout canoes—40% of Japan’s total excavated examples—and controlled waterways on the [[:en:Katori Sea|Katori Sea]], suggesting its significant influence. Between the 4th and 5th centuries, prominent Kofuns such as {{ill|Shakushi Kofun|ja|しゃくし塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakushi Kofun|1=Shakushi Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11269068}} and {{ill|Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|三ノ分目大塚山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|qq=}} were constructed.<ref>The use of [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] and {{ill|Sarcophaguss|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarcophaguss|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}} in these tumuli indicates high-Ranking individuals.</ref><ref>Examples of Nagamochigata Sarcophaguss, considered royal burials, are rare in the Kantō region.</ref> Along with {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}}, the Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko likely controlled an expansive area described as "Ō Unakami Kuni," stretching across modern Chiba, Ibaraki, Saitama, and Tokyo.
By the 6th century, the {{ill|Joyama Tomb No.1 (Katori)|lt=Joyama Tomb No.1 (Katori)|lt=Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|ja|城山1号墳 (香取市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|qq=}} and {{ill|Omaekizuka Kofun|ja|御前鬼塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omaekizuka Kofun|1=Omaekizuka Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488824}} were built, while the {{ill|Kinouchi Temple Ruins|ja|木内廃寺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinouchi Temple Ruins|qq=}} appeared during the transition from the Kokuzō system to the Ritsuryō system in the 7th century.
== Genealogy == The Kokuzō lineage includes the Hisatsuki family ([[:en:Kabane|Kabane]]: {{ill|Atai|ja|直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atai|1=Atai (kabane)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581705}}) and the Oda Hihafuri family (also titled Atai). Records indicate descendants served administrative roles and were acknowledged in later periods. Oda Hihafuri Shingō, noted in the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsōin documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, and Oda Hihafuri Tokutōji, mentioned in the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]], highlight their continued prominence.
Additionally, the Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō appears connected to [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]] as a clan deity, with the Oda Hihafuri family suspected as early ritual practitioners.<ref>Katori Shrine’s origins link to these clans.</ref>
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Raijin Shrine (Asahi City)|ja|雷神社 (旭市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raijin Shrine (Asahi City)|qq=}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/kenkyu/boso/simo.html Ancient Tumuli of Shimotsu Unakami Kuni]

This region boasted the [[:en:Kuriyama River|Kuriyama River]] and the {{ill|Tsubakimi Lake|ja|椿海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsubakimi Lake|qq=}} water systems, yielding 80 dugout canoes—40% of Japan’s total excavated examples—and controlled waterways on the [[:en:Katori Sea|Katori Sea]], suggesting its significant influence. Between the 4th and 5th centuries, prominent Kofuns such as {{ill|Shakushi Kofun|ja|しゃくし塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakushi Kofun|1=Shakushi Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11269068}} and {{ill|Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|三ノ分目大塚山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|qq=}} were constructed.<ref>The use of [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] and {{ill|Sarcophaguss|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarcophaguss|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}} in these tumuli indicates high-Ranking individuals.</ref><ref>Examples of Nagamochigata Sarcophaguss, considered royal burials, are rare in the Kantō region.</ref> Along with {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}}, the Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko likely controlled an expansive area described as "Ō Unakami Kuni," stretching across modern Chiba, Ibaraki, Saitama, and Tokyo.
By the 6th century, the {{ill|Joyama Tomb No.1 (Katori)|lt=Joyama Tomb No.1 (Katori)|lt=Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|ja|城山1号墳 (香取市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|qq=}} and {{ill|Omaekizuka Kofun|ja|御前鬼塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omaekizuka Kofun|1=Omaekizuka Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488824}} were built, while the {{ill|Kinouchi Temple Ruins|ja|木内廃寺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinouchi Temple Ruins|qq=}} appeared during the transition from the Kokuzō system to the Ritsuryō system in the 7th century.
== Genealogy == The Kokuzō lineage includes the Hisatsuki family ([[:en:Kabane|Kabane]]: {{ill|Atai|ja|直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atai|1=Atai (kabane)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581705}}) and the Oda Hihafuri family (also titled Atai). Records indicate descendants served administrative roles and were acknowledged in later periods. Oda Hihafuri Shingō, noted in the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsōin documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, and Oda Hihafuri Tokutōji, mentioned in the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]], highlight their continued prominence.
Additionally, the Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō appears connected to [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]] as a clan deity, with the Oda Hihafuri family suspected as early ritual practitioners.<ref>Katori Shrine’s origins link to these clans.</ref>
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Raijin Shrine (Asahi City)|ja|雷神社 (旭市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raijin Shrine (Asahi City)|qq=}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/kenkyu/boso/simo.html Ancient Tumuli of Shimotsu Unakami Kuni]

{{wikidata link|Q11361123}} [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:History of Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shimōsa Province]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:下海上国造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:下海上国造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:下海上国造]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11361123]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (下海上国造, also known as Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō) governed the eastern portion of the later [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] of [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]], covering present-day [[:en:Chōshi|Chōshi]], [[:en:Asahi, Chiba|Asahi]], and [[:en:Katori District|Katori District]] in [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]]. The title is also written as 下菟上国造.
== Overview == According to the "[[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]]" ("Record of Ancient Matters"), the position of Kokuzō was established during the reign of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Ōjin]] when Hisatsuki no Atae, a descendant of the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}}, was appointed as Kokuzō. The "[[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]" also identifies {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天之菩卑能命|1=Ame no Hohi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hohi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノホヒ}}’s child, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|1=Takehi-Nateru|zh|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehiratori no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|qq=}}, as the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō.<ref>The "Kojiki" notes that Takehiratori no Mikoto was the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō, among others, such as the {{ill|Izumo clan|lt=Izumo clan|lt=Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]].</ref>
This region boasted the [[:en:Kuriyama River|Kuriyama River]] and the {{ill|Tsubakimi Lake|ja|椿海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsubakimi Lake|qq=}} water systems, yielding 80 dugout canoes—40% of Japan’s total excavated examples—and controlled waterways on the [[:en:Katori Sea|Katori Sea]], suggesting its significant influence. Between the 4th and 5th centuries, prominent Kofuns such as {{ill|Shakushi Kofun|ja|しゃくし塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakushi Kofun|1=Shakushi Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11269068}} and {{ill|Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|三ノ分目大塚山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|qq=}} were constructed.<ref>The use of [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] and {{ill|Sarcophaguss|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarcophaguss|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}} in these tumuli indicates high-Ranking individuals.</ref><ref>Examples of Nagamochigata Sarcophaguss, considered royal burials, are rare in the Kantō region.</ref> Along with {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}}, the Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko likely controlled an expansive area described as "Ō Unakami Kuni," stretching across modern Chiba, Ibaraki, Saitama, and Tokyo.
By the 6th century, the {{ill|Joyama Tomb No.1 (Katori)|lt=Joyama Tomb No.1 (Katori)|lt=Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|ja|城山1号墳 (香取市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|qq=}} and {{ill|Omaekizuka Kofun|ja|御前鬼塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omaekizuka Kofun|1=Omaekizuka Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488824}} were built, while the {{ill|Kinouchi Temple Ruins|ja|木内廃寺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinouchi Temple Ruins|qq=}} appeared during the transition from the Kokuzō system to the Ritsuryō system in the 7th century.
== Genealogy == The Kokuzō lineage includes the Hisatsuki family ([[:en:Kabane|Kabane]]: {{ill|Atai|ja|直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atai|1=Atai (kabane)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581705}}) and the Oda Hihafuri family (also titled Atai). Records indicate descendants served administrative roles and were acknowledged in later periods. Oda Hihafuri Shingō, noted in the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsōin documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, and Oda Hihafuri Tokutōji, mentioned in the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]], highlight their continued prominence.
Additionally, the Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō appears connected to [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]] as a clan deity, with the Oda Hihafuri family suspected as early ritual practitioners.<ref>Katori Shrine’s origins link to these clans.</ref>
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Raijin Shrine (Asahi City)|ja|雷神社 (旭市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raijin Shrine (Asahi City)|qq=}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:下海上国造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:下海上国造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:下海上国造]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11361123]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|'''Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko'''|下海上国造||Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō}} governed the eastern portion of the later [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] of [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]], covering present-day [[:en:Chōshi|Chōshi]], [[:en:Asahi, Chiba|Asahi]], and [[:en:Katori District|Katori District]] in [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]]. The title is also written as 下菟上国造.
== Overview == According to the "[[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]]" ("Record of Ancient Matters"), the position of Kokuzō was established during the reign of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Ōjin]] when Hisatsuki no Atae, a descendant of the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}}, was appointed as Kokuzō. The "[[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]" also identifies {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天之菩卑能命|1=Ame no Hohi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hohi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノホヒ}}’s child, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|1=Takehi-Nateru|zh|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehiratori no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|qq=}}, as the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō.<ref>The "Kojiki" notes that Takehiratori no Mikoto was the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō, among others, such as the {{ill|Izumo clan|lt=Izumo clan|lt=Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]].</ref>
This region boasted the [[:en:Kuriyama River|Kuriyama River]] and the {{ill|Tsubakimi Lake|ja|椿海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsubakimi Lake|qq=}} water systems, yielding 80 dugout canoes—40% of Japan’s total excavated examples—and controlled waterways on the [[:en:Katori Sea|Katori Sea]], suggesting its significant influence. Between the 4th and 5th centuries, prominent Kofuns such as {{ill|Shakushi Kofun|ja|しゃくし塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakushi Kofun|1=Shakushi Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11269068}} and {{ill|Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|三ノ分目大塚山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|qq=}} were constructed.<ref>The use of [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] and {{ill|Sarcophaguss|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarcophaguss|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}} in these tumuli indicates high-Ranking individuals.</ref><ref>Examples of Nagamochigata Sarcophaguss, considered royal burials, are rare in the Kantō region.</ref> Along with {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}}, the Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko likely controlled an expansive area described as "Ō Unakami Kuni," stretching across modern Chiba, Ibaraki, Saitama, and Tokyo.
By the 6th century, the {{ill|Joyama Tomb No.1 (Katori)|lt=Joyama Tomb No.1 (Katori)|lt=Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|ja|城山1号墳 (香取市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|qq=}} and {{ill|Omaekizuka Kofun|ja|御前鬼塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omaekizuka Kofun|1=Omaekizuka Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488824}} were built, while the {{ill|Kinouchi Temple Ruins|ja|木内廃寺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinouchi Temple Ruins|qq=}} appeared during the transition from the Kokuzō system to the Ritsuryō system in the 7th century.
== Genealogy == The Kokuzō lineage includes the Hisatsuki family ([[:en:Kabane|Kabane]]: {{ill|Atai|ja|直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atai|1=Atai (kabane)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581705}}) and the Oda Hihafuri family (also titled Atai). Records indicate descendants served administrative roles and were acknowledged in later periods. Oda Hihafuri Shingō, noted in the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsōin documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, and Oda Hihafuri Tokutōji, mentioned in the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]], highlight their continued prominence.
Additionally, the Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō appears connected to [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]] as a clan deity, with the Oda Hihafuri family suspected as early ritual practitioners.<ref>Katori Shrine’s origins link to these clans.</ref>
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Raijin Shrine (Asahi City)|ja|雷神社 (旭市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raijin Shrine (Asahi City)|qq=}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/kenkyu/boso/simo.html Ancient Tumuli of Shimotsu Unakami Kuni]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|下海上国造|version=94790894|comment=Imported full ja history}}

The title is also written as 下菟上国造.
== Overview == According to the "{{ill|Kujiki|en|Kujiki|WD=Q2716751|lt=Sendai Kuji Hongi}}" ("Record of Ancient Matters"), the position of Kokuzō was established during the reign of Emperor {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Ōjin}} when Hisatsuki no Atae, a descendant of the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}}, was appointed as Kokuzō. The "{{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}}" also identifies {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天之菩卑能命|1=Ame no Hohi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hohi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノホヒ}}’s child, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|1=Takehi-Nateru|zh|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehiratori no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|qq=}}, as the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō.<ref>The "Kojiki" notes that Takehiratori no Mikoto was the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō, among others, such as the {{ill|Izumo clan|lt=Izumo clan|lt=Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province}}.</ref>
This region boasted the {{ill|Kuriyama River|en|Kuriyama River|WD=Q6446101|lt=Kuriyama River}} and the {{ill|Tsubakimi Lake|ja|椿海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsubakimi Lake|qq=}} water systems, yielding 80 dugout canoes—40% of Japan’s total excavated examples—and controlled waterways on the {{ill|Katori Sea|en|Katori Sea|WD=Q11667182|lt=Katori Sea}}, suggesting its significant influence. Between the 4th and 5th centuries, prominent Kofuns such as {{ill|Shakushi Kofun|ja|しゃくし塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakushi Kofun|1=Shakushi Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11269068}} and {{ill|Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|三ノ分目大塚山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|qq=}} were constructed.<ref>The use of {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}} and {{ill|Sarcophaguss|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarcophaguss|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}} in these tumuli indicates high-Ranking individuals.</ref><ref>Examples of Nagamochigata Sarcophaguss, considered royal burials, are rare in the Kantō region.</ref> Along with {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}}, the Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko likely controlled an expansive area described as "Ō Unakami Kuni," stretching across modern Chiba, Ibaraki, Saitama, and Tokyo.
By the 6th century, the {{ill|Joyama Tomb No.1 (Katori)|lt=Joyama Tomb No.1 (Katori)|lt=Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|ja|城山1号墳 (香取市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|qq=}} and {{ill|Omaekizuka Kofun|ja|御前鬼塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omaekizuka Kofun|1=Omaekizuka Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488824}} were built, while the {{ill|Kinouchi Temple Ruins|ja|木内廃寺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinouchi Temple Ruins|qq=}} appeared during the transition from the Kokuzō system to the Ritsuryō system in the 7th century.
== Genealogy == The Kokuzō lineage includes the Hisatsuki family ({{ill|Kabane|en|Kabane|WD=Q1664099|lt=Kabane}}: {{ill|Atai|ja|直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atai|1=Atai (kabane)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581705}}) and the Oda Hihafuri family (also titled Atai). Records indicate descendants served administrative roles and were acknowledged in later periods. Oda Hihafuri Shingō, noted in the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsōin documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, and Oda Hihafuri Tokutōji, mentioned in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|WD=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū}}, highlight their continued prominence.
Additionally, the Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō appears connected to {{ill|Katori Shrine|en|Katori Shrine|WD=Q372380|lt=Katori Shrine}} as a clan deity, with the Oda Hihafuri family suspected as early ritual practitioners.<ref>Katori Shrine’s origins link to these clans.</ref>
