This region boasted the {{ill|Kuriyama River|en|Kuriyama River|WD=Q6446101|lt=Kuriyama River}} and the {{ill|Tsubakimi Lake|ja|椿海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsubakimi Lake|qq=}} water systems, yielding 80 dugout canoes—40% of Japan’s total excavated examples—and controlled waterways on the {{ill|Katori Sea|en|Katori Sea|WD=Q11667182|lt=Katori Sea}}, suggesting its significant influence. Between the 4th and 5th centuries, prominent Kofuns such as {{ill|Shakushi Kofun|ja|しゃくし塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakushi Kofun|1=Shakushi Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11269068}} and {{ill|Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|三ノ分目大塚山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|qq=}} were constructed.<ref>The use of {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}} and {{ill|Sarcophaguss|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarcophaguss|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}} in these tumuli indicates high-Ranking individuals.</ref><ref>Examples of Nagamochigata Sarcophaguss, considered royal burials, are rare in the Kantō region.</ref> Along with {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}}, the Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko likely controlled an expansive area described as "Ō Unakami Kuni," stretching across modern Chiba, Ibaraki, Saitama, and Tokyo.
By the 6th century, the {{ill|Joyama Tomb No.1 (Katori)|lt=Joyama Tomb No.1 (Katori)|lt=Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|ja|城山1号墳 (香取市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|qq=}} and {{ill|Omaekizuka Kofun|ja|御前鬼塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omaekizuka Kofun|1=Omaekizuka Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488824}} were built, while the {{ill|Kinouchi Temple Ruins|ja|木内廃寺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinouchi Temple Ruins|qq=}} appeared during the transition from the Kokuzō system to the Ritsuryō system in the 7th century.
== Genealogy == The Kokuzō lineage includes the Hisatsuki family ({{ill|Kabane|en|Kabane|WD=Q1664099|lt=Kabane}}: {{ill|Atai|ja|直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atai|1=Atai (kabane)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581705}}) and the Oda Hihafuri family (also titled Atai). Records indicate descendants served administrative roles and were acknowledged in later periods. Oda Hihafuri Shingō, noted in the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsōin documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, and Oda Hihafuri Tokutōji, mentioned in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|WD=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū}}, highlight their continued prominence.
Additionally, the Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō appears connected to {{ill|Katori Shrine|en|Katori Shrine|WD=Q372380|lt=Katori Shrine}} as a clan deity, with the Oda Hihafuri family suspected as early ritual practitioners.<ref>Katori Shrine’s origins link to these clans.</ref>
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Raijin Shrine (Asahi City)|ja|雷神社 (旭市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raijin Shrine (Asahi City)|qq=}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/kenkyu/boso/simo.html Ancient Tumuli of Shimotsu Unakami Kuni]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:下海上国造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:下海上国造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:下海上国造]]
{{nihongo|'''Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko'''|下海上国造||Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō}} governed the eastern portion of the later {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system}} of {{ill|Shimōsa Province|en|Shimōsa Province|WD=Q1053695|lt=Shimōsa Province}}, covering present-day {{ill|Chōshi|en|Chōshi|WD=Q694317|lt=Chōshi}}, {{ill|Asahi, Chiba|en|Asahi, Chiba|WD=Q637868|lt=Asahi}}, and {{ill|Katori District|en|Katori District|WD=Q780284|lt=Katori District}} in {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture}}. The title is also written as 下菟上国造.
== Overview == According to the "{{ill|Kujiki|en|Kujiki|WD=Q2716751|lt=Sendai Kuji Hongi}}" ("Record of Ancient Matters"), the position of Kokuzō was established during the reign of Emperor {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Ōjin}} when Hisatsuki no Atae, a descendant of the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}}, was appointed as Kokuzō. The "{{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}}" also identifies {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天之菩卑能命|1=Ame no Hohi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hohi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノホヒ}}’s child, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|1=Takehi-Nateru|zh|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehiratori no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|qq=}}, as the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō.<ref>The "Kojiki" notes that Takehiratori no Mikoto was the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō, among others, such as the {{ill|Izumo clan|lt=Izumo clan|lt=Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province}}.</ref>
This region boasted the {{ill|Kuriyama River|en|Kuriyama River|WD=Q6446101|lt=Kuriyama River}} and the {{ill|Tsubakimi Lake|ja|椿海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsubakimi Lake|qq=}} water systems, yielding 80 dugout canoes—40% of Japan’s total excavated examples—and controlled waterways on the {{ill|Katori Sea|en|Katori Sea|WD=Q11667182|lt=Katori Sea}}, suggesting its significant influence. Between the 4th and 5th centuries, prominent Kofuns such as {{ill|Shakushi Kofun|ja|しゃくし塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakushi Kofun|1=Shakushi Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11269068}} and {{ill|Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|三ノ分目大塚山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|qq=}} were constructed.<ref>The use of {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}} and {{ill|Sarcophaguss|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarcophaguss|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}} in these tumuli indicates high-Ranking individuals.</ref><ref>Examples of Nagamochigata Sarcophaguss, considered royal burials, are rare in the Kantō region.</ref> Along with {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}}, the Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko likely controlled an expansive area described as "Ō Unakami Kuni," stretching across modern Chiba, Ibaraki, Saitama, and Tokyo.
By the 6th century, the {{ill|Joyama Tomb No.1 (Katori)|lt=Joyama Tomb No.1 (Katori)|lt=Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|ja|城山1号墳 (香取市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|qq=}} and {{ill|Omaekizuka Kofun|ja|御前鬼塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omaekizuka Kofun|1=Omaekizuka Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488824}} were built, while the {{ill|Kinouchi Temple Ruins|ja|木内廃寺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinouchi Temple Ruins|qq=}} appeared during the transition from the Kokuzō system to the Ritsuryō system in the 7th century.
== Genealogy == The Kokuzō lineage includes the Hisatsuki family ({{ill|Kabane|en|Kabane|WD=Q1664099|lt=Kabane}}: {{ill|Atai|ja|直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atai|1=Atai (kabane)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581705}}) and the Oda Hihafuri family (also titled Atai). Records indicate descendants served administrative roles and were acknowledged in later periods. Oda Hihafuri Shingō, noted in the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsōin documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, and Oda Hihafuri Tokutōji, mentioned in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|WD=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū}}, highlight their continued prominence.
Additionally, the Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō appears connected to {{ill|Katori Shrine|en|Katori Shrine|WD=Q372380|lt=Katori Shrine}} as a clan deity, with the Oda Hihafuri family suspected as early ritual practitioners.<ref>Katori Shrine’s origins link to these clans.</ref>
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Raijin Shrine (Asahi City)|ja|雷神社 (旭市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raijin Shrine (Asahi City)|qq=}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/kenkyu/boso/simo.html Ancient Tumuli of Shimotsu Unakami Kuni]

This region boasted the {{ill|Kuriyama River|en|Kuriyama River|WD=Q6446101|lt=Kuriyama River|1=Kuriyama River}} and the {{ill|Tsubakimi Lake|ja|椿海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsubakimi Lake|qq=}} water systems, yielding 80 dugout canoes—40% of Japan’s total excavated examples—and controlled waterways on the {{ill|Katori Sea|en|Katori Sea|WD=Q11667182|lt=Katori Sea|1=Katori Sea}}, suggesting its significant influence. Between the 4th and 5th centuries, prominent Kofuns such as {{ill|Shakushi Kofun|ja|しゃくし塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakushi Kofun|1=Q11269068|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11269068}} and {{ill|Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|三ノ分目大塚山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|qq=}} were constructed.<ref>The use of {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}} and {{ill|Sarcophaguss|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarcophaguss|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}} in these tumuli indicates high-Ranking individuals.</ref><ref>Examples of Nagamochigata Sarcophaguss, considered royal burials, are rare in the Kantō region.</ref> Along with {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}}, the Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko likely controlled an expansive area described as "Ō Unakami Kuni," stretching across modern Chiba, Ibaraki, Saitama, and Tokyo.
By the 6th century, the {{ill|Joyama Tomb No.1 (Katori)|lt=Joyama Tomb No.1 (Katori)|lt=Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|ja|城山1号墳 (香取市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|qq=}} and {{ill|Omaekizuka Kofun|ja|御前鬼塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omaekizuka Kofun|1=Q11488824|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488824}} were built, while the {{ill|Kinouchi Temple Ruins|ja|木内廃寺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinouchi Temple Ruins|qq=}} appeared during the transition from the Kokuzō system to the Ritsuryō system in the 7th century.
== Genealogy == The Kokuzō lineage includes the Hisatsuki family ({{ill|Kabane|en|Kabane|WD=Q1664099|lt=Kabane|1=Kabane}}: {{ill|Atai|ja|直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atai|1=Q11581705|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581705}}) and the Oda Hihafuri family (also titled Atai). Records indicate descendants served administrative roles and were acknowledged in later periods. Oda Hihafuri Shingō, noted in the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsōin documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, and Oda Hihafuri Tokutōji, mentioned in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|WD=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū|1=Man'yōshū}}, highlight their continued prominence.
Additionally, the Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō appears connected to {{ill|Katori Shrine|en|Katori Shrine|WD=Q372380|lt=Katori Shrine|1=Katori Jingū}} as a clan deity, with the Oda Hihafuri family suspected as early ritual practitioners.<ref>Katori Shrine’s origins link to these clans.</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:下海上国造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:下海上国造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:下海上国造]]
{{nihongo|'''Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko'''|下海上国造||Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō}} governed the eastern portion of the later {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō}} of {{ill|Shimōsa Province|en|Shimōsa Province|WD=Q1053695|lt=Shimōsa Province|1=Shimōsa Province}}, covering present-day {{ill|Chōshi|en|Chōshi|WD=Q694317|lt=Chōshi|1=Chōshi}}, {{ill|Asahi, Chiba|en|Asahi, Chiba|WD=Q637868|lt=Asahi|1=Asahi, Chiba}}, and {{ill|Katori District|en|Katori District|WD=Q780284|lt=Katori District|1=Katori District}} in {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}}. The title is also written as 下菟上国造.
== Overview == According to the "{{ill|Kujiki|en|Kujiki|WD=Q2716751|lt=Sendai Kuji Hongi|1=Kujiki}}" ("Record of Ancient Matters"), the position of Kokuzō was established during the reign of Emperor {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}} when Hisatsuki no Atae, a descendant of the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}}, was appointed as Kokuzō. The "{{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}" also identifies {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天之菩卑能命|1=Ame no Hohi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hohi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノホヒ}}’s child, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|1=Takehi-Nateru|zh|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehiratori no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|qq=}}, as the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō.<ref>The "Kojiki" notes that Takehiratori no Mikoto was the ancestor of Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō, among others, such as the {{ill|Izumo clan|lt=Izumo clan|lt=Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province|1=Musashi Province}}.</ref>
This region boasted the {{ill|Kuriyama River|en|Kuriyama River|WD=Q6446101|lt=Kuriyama River|1=Kuriyama River}} and the {{ill|Tsubakimi Lake|ja|椿海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsubakimi Lake|qq=}} water systems, yielding 80 dugout canoes—40% of Japan’s total excavated examples—and controlled waterways on the {{ill|Katori Sea|en|Katori Sea|WD=Q11667182|lt=Katori Sea|1=Katori Sea}}, suggesting its significant influence. Between the 4th and 5th centuries, prominent Kofuns such as {{ill|Shakushi Kofun|ja|しゃくし塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakushi Kofun|1=Q11269068|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11269068}} and {{ill|Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|三ノ分目大塚山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannobunme Ōtsukayama Kofun|qq=}} were constructed.<ref>The use of {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}} and {{ill|Sarcophaguss|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarcophaguss|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}} in these tumuli indicates high-Ranking individuals.</ref><ref>Examples of Nagamochigata Sarcophaguss, considered royal burials, are rare in the Kantō region.</ref> Along with {{ill|Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|1=Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}}, the Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko likely controlled an expansive area described as "Ō Unakami Kuni," stretching across modern Chiba, Ibaraki, Saitama, and Tokyo.
By the 6th century, the {{ill|Joyama Tomb No.1 (Katori)|lt=Joyama Tomb No.1 (Katori)|lt=Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|ja|城山1号墳 (香取市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori)|qq=}} and {{ill|Omaekizuka Kofun|ja|御前鬼塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omaekizuka Kofun|1=Q11488824|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488824}} were built, while the {{ill|Kinouchi Temple Ruins|ja|木内廃寺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinouchi Temple Ruins|qq=}} appeared during the transition from the Kokuzō system to the Ritsuryō system in the 7th century.
== Genealogy == The Kokuzō lineage includes the Hisatsuki family ({{ill|Kabane|en|Kabane|WD=Q1664099|lt=Kabane|1=Kabane}}: {{ill|Atai|ja|直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atai|1=Q11581705|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581705}}) and the Oda Hihafuri family (also titled Atai). Records indicate descendants served administrative roles and were acknowledged in later periods. Oda Hihafuri Shingō, noted in the {{ill|Shōsōin documents|ja|正倉院文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsōin documents|1=Shōsōin Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545362}}, and Oda Hihafuri Tokutōji, mentioned in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|WD=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū|1=Man'yōshū}}, highlight their continued prominence.
Additionally, the Shimotsu Unakami Kokuzō appears connected to {{ill|Katori Shrine|en|Katori Shrine|WD=Q372380|lt=Katori Shrine|1=Katori Jingū}} as a clan deity, with the Oda Hihafuri family suspected as early ritual practitioners.<ref>Katori Shrine’s origins link to these clans.</ref>
